Financial services regulators face this acutely: advisory/trading information barrier compliance cannot be achieved with access control alone for agentic systems. Regulators should: (1) update information barrier guidance to address multi-agent systems and path-dependent violations; (2) require runtime governance infrastructure (not just pre-deployment controls) for agentic AI in financial services; (3) mandate that organisations demonstrate path-level audit trails for agent operations; (4) develop examination procedures for testing path-dependent compliance in agentic deployments.
advice.prompt: If a supervised entity deployed autonomous AI agents that could independently initiate transactions, access multiple data systems, and communicate externally — all individually within permitted parameters — does your current regulatory framework have the tools to detect violations that emerge from the combination of those actions?

advice.headline: Mandate provenance for targets, labels, and proxies — not just models and datasets
advice.body: Current disclosure regimes focus on model architecture, training data lineage, and post-hoc metrics. That misses the upstream trust problem: many harmful systems are faithfully optimizing a weak or socially loaded proxy. Regulators should require firms to document how consequential targets were constructed, why proxies were chosen, what dissent or disagreement existed, and when those targets must be revisited.
advice.prompt: Does your current AI documentation regime reveal whether a firm is optimizing the wrong target, or only whether it is optimizing that target efficiently?

advice.headline: Supervise with sampled proof drills, not paper oversight attestations
advice.body: Oversight mandates are weak if firms can satisfy them with role descriptions, workflow diagrams, and high-level policy claims. Supervisors should request one recent AI-influenced case and require a reconstructable evidence packet within a risk-scaled timeframe. That single test exposes whether human oversight, logging, version control, escalation, and monitoring are real operating capabilities or narrative placeholders.
advice.prompt: What would happen if your examination program treated “show me one real case” as the primary test of AI governance maturity?

advice.headline: The accountability horizon extends beyond your regulatory reach — mandate provenance disclosure to make it legible
advice.body: Cobbe et al. (p135) establish that the accountability horizon for AI supply chains extends significantly beyond the regulatory reach of any single jurisdiction. Foundation model developers, training data providers, and evaluation benchmark creators sit outside domestic regulatory jurisdiction — yet financial institutions bear accountability for AI system failures that may originate with these actors. The structural mismatch is not solvable by extending domestic regulation extraterritorially — it requires making the accountability horizon legible through mandatory provenance disclosure. Regulators should require financial institutions to document: the foundation models used, the training data provenance where known, the evaluation benchmarks relied upon, and the contractual arrangements with upstream AI service providers. This disclosure does not create liability for upstream actors — it creates visibility for the institution's risk management and for supervisory assessment of where the actual risks in the AI supply chain lie.
advice.prompt: Do you have visibility into the accountability horizon of the AI systems used by regulated financial institutions — which foundation models, training data sources, and evaluation benchmarks they depend on? If not, your supervisory framework has a structural blind spot for the most consequential AI supply chain risks.

advice.headline: Homewood et al.'s three-question diagnostic should be the standard for evaluating AI audit quality across your supervised population
advice.body: Homewood et al. (p136) conducted the first empirical study of what third-party AI compliance reviews actually assess — and found a systematic pattern of high scope, low depth, low independence. The three-question diagnostic (who is reviewing, what are they reviewing, what can they actually see?) should become your framework for evaluating whether any AI audit claim by a regulated entity meets minimum quality standards. If a financial institution claims its AI systems have been reviewed by an external party, you should be able to assess whether that review provides genuine assurance or merely the appearance of it. Regulators should require disclosure of: the auditor's independence from the auditee, the technical depth of the assessment, and the access the auditor had to production systems and relevant documentation. Without this, you cannot evaluate whether AI audit claims in your supervised population are credible.
advice.prompt: When regulated entities present third-party AI audit reports, do you assess the depth and independence of those reviews — or do you accept scope claims at face value? If a review fails the three-question diagnostic (high scope, low depth, low independence), what is your supervisory response?

advice.headline: AEF-1 minimum operating conditions provide the concrete definition of 'independent evaluation' that regulation currently lacks
advice.body: The AEF-1 framework (p131) defines five minimum operating conditions for independent third-party frontier AI evaluations: evaluator independence, technical access standards, information security, transparency requirements, and conflict of interest management. These conditions exist because the concept of 'independent AI evaluation' has been used loosely — organisations commissioning evaluations cannot distinguish genuine independence from cosponsored research that produces favourable results. For regulators, AEF-1 provides a concrete reference point: when the EU AI Act requires conformity assessment by 'independent bodies,' or when MAS AIRG requires 'external assessment,' those requirements should be interpreted against AEF-1 minimum conditions. An evaluation that fails AEF-1 should not satisfy regulatory expectations for independent assessment. Regulators should reference AEF-1 in guidance documents as the minimum standard for what 'independent' means in the context of AI evaluation.
advice.prompt: When your regulatory framework requires 'independent' AI assessment, do you have a definition of what 'independent' means in technical and structural terms? If an organisation presents an AI evaluation conducted by a firm with significant commercial relationships with the AI developer, does your framework have a basis for saying that evaluation is not independent?

advice.headline: MAS TPRM and APRA CPS 230/234 were not built for AI software supply chain risk — the gap is systemic
advice.body: The MAS Consultation Paper on TPRM (March 2026) and APRA's CPS 230 and CPS 234 are grounded in traditional outsourcing and operational risk frameworks. They focus on vendor reliability, service continuity, and security controls at the organizational level. Neither framework was designed for the structural reality documented in this research: AI systems are built from modular software components that pass through many hands, no single actor has complete visibility, and accountability is structurally dislocated across supply chain actors. The LiteLLM incident would not have been prevented by either framework because both assume the relationship with the vendor is the governance boundary, not the vendor's software supply chain. Regulators should develop AI-specific third-party risk management requirements that mandate: AI BOMs for all AI systems used in regulated activities, sub-processor disclosure requirements analogous to GDPR sub-processor requirements, and continuous monitoring of AI system provenance (not just point-in-time due diligence). The MAS AI Risk Management Guidelines (which reference AI) provide the foundation; they need explicit supply chain provisions.
advice.prompt: Do your current AI third-party risk management guidelines require visibility into the AI vendor's software supply chain? If not, what would it take to add AI BOM requirements to your framework?

advice.headline: Mandatory AI BOMs for high-stakes AI deployment should be the minimum regulatory ask
advice.body: The EU AI Act is moving toward mandatory transparency documentation for high-risk AI systems. The SPDX 3.0 AI BOM standard provides the technical mechanism to deliver this. Financial regulators should mandate AI BOM requirements for all AI systems deployed in regulated activities above a risk threshold. The AI BOM should at minimum include: foundation model identification and version, training data provenance (to the extent known), fine-tuning procedure documentation, dependency tree (AI-specific and software), and build/deployment provenance. This is not a novel requirement — it is the AI-specific extension of SBOM requirements already being implemented in software supply chain security. The business case is straightforward: AI BOMs enable rapid incident response (as demonstrated by the LiteLLM case), support regulatory examination, and reduce the asymmetry of information between regulated institutions and AI vendors. The implementation gap is small; the regulatory signal needed is large.
advice.prompt: At what risk threshold should AI BOMs be mandated in your jurisdiction? What body of regulated entities would be affected, and what implementation timeline is realistic?

advice.headline: Mandate epistemic-status disclosure for AI trust claims — not just metrics and model cards
advice.body: Regulatory documentation regimes still over-focus on what a system claims to do and under-focus on what kind of claim is being made. This session points to a sharper supervisory requirement: firms should disclose whether material trust claims are grounded in direct observation, socially constructed labels, indirect proxies, or untested assumptions, and explain how each claim degrades in deployment. That would expose weak foundations early and make it harder for institutions to pass off proxy metrics as settled truth. It also aligns oversight with what matters in practice: whether a trust claim remains checkable after deployment, not whether it looked neat at approval time.
advice.prompt: Does your current AI documentation regime reveal which claims are assumptions and proxies, or does it allow firms to present all trust claims as if they had the same evidentiary status?

advice.headline: Negligence liability for AI is shifting from individual action review to systemic governance adequacy — update your supervisory expectations
advice.body: Courts in multiple jurisdictions are moving toward a 'reasonable AI system' standard for negligence claims. The question is no longer 'was any individual AI action adequately reviewed by a human before it caused harm?' — an unanswerable question for systems taking thousands of actions per second. The question is becoming: 'did the organisation have adequate governance, oversight architecture, monitoring, and rollback capability for the AI systems it deployed?' This is a structural shift in liability that changes what directors and officers need to oversee. Financial regulators should update their supervisory expectations to specify: what adequate AI governance architecture looks like, what monitoring systems must be in place, what documentation creates court-defensible evidence of governance adequacy, and how oversight responsibilities map to specific officer accountabilities.
advice.prompt: Does your supervisory framework specify what adequate AI governance architecture looks like in enough detail that a court would find it persuasive if an organisation were sued for negligence related to an AI system? If not, what should it specify?

advice.headline: Mandate epistemic-status disclosure for AI trust claims — proxy is not ground truth
advice.body: Current AI documentation regimes allow organisations to present benchmark performance, model cards, and audit results as equivalent trust signals — even when they measure fundamentally different things. This research points to a sharper supervisory requirement: firms should disclose, for material trust claims, whether each claim is grounded in direct observation, socially constructed labels, indirect proxies, or untested assumptions — and explain how each degrades in deployment conditions. This would expose weak foundations early and make it harder for institutions to pass off proxy metrics as settled truth. For example: training accuracy = proxy (in-distribution), regulatory certification of process = socially constructed, alignment testing = direct observation (but only for tested contexts). Epistemic status disclosure would transform how trust claims are evaluated — from whether they sound credible to whether they have the right kind of evidence.
advice.prompt: Does your AI documentation regime distinguish between trust claims that are directly observed, socially constructed, or based on indirect proxies? If all claims look equally credible in documentation, you are allowing firms to obscure what they don't actually know.

advice.headline: Distinguish certifiable-once properties from continuous-monitoring properties in your framework
advice.body: Not all trust properties can be certified at a point in time. Accuracy, calibration, and fairness drift over time; they require continuous monitoring infrastructure. Only structural properties — training data governance, architectural human override capability, data lineage completeness — can be meaningfully certified once. If your regulatory framework allows point-in-time certification of dynamic properties, you are creating compliance theatre. Specify which properties require continuous monitoring, mandate the infrastructure for it, and audit for the existence and effectiveness of drift detection rather than relying on historical model performance snapshots.
advice.prompt: Does your AI regulatory framework distinguish between properties that can be certified once (structural) versus those requiring continuous monitoring (dynamic)? If not, certified models may be trustworthy today and untrustworthy within weeks.

advice.headline: The automation paradox invalidates volumetric human-in-the-loop requirements
advice.body: Bainbridge's automation paradox proves that naive human-in-the-loop (humans review every AI action) is cognitively impossible at scale. Human oversight quality degrades precisely when AI output volumes are highest. Your regulatory framework's "meaningful human oversight" requirement is not satisfied by specifying the number or seniority of human reviewers. It requires specifying the architectural conditions under which human oversight is meaningful: (1) the right to understand AI decisions; (2) the capability to intervene at consequential decision points; (3) the ability to trace responsibility chain. Rewrite your human-in-the-loop requirements to specify architectural conditions, not personnel ratios.
advice.prompt: Does your human-in-the-loop requirement specify per-action review (which is infeasible at scale) or architectural control provisions (which are feasible)? If the former, your requirement cannot be met and will produce compliance theatre.

advice.headline: Follow Singapore's MindForge model — mandate industry-regulator co-development for financial services AI governance
advice.body: Singapore's MAS has demonstrated that industry-regulator co-development (MindForge: 24 institutions + MAS over multiple phases) produces implementable AI governance. Pure regulatory frameworks tend to be either too abstract or too prescriptive. Pure industry self-governance tends to be self-serving. The Singapore model distributes innovation costs, creates shared ownership, and produces practical outputs (operationalisation handbook, case studies). Other jurisdictions should study this model and replicate it: convene industry consortia, provide regulatory coordination without dictating solutions, and require practical outputs (case studies, operationalisation guides) as conditions of the process.
advice.prompt: Is your AI regulatory development process designed for co-development with industry, or is it a top-down mandate? What would change if you convened an industry consortium model similar to Singapore's MindForge?
